coredata.dc:title: Interaction of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles with 3-N-(N′-methylacetamidino) benzanthrone: A temperature dependent fluorescence quenching study
coredata.dc:creator: Bharathi, D.
Siddlingeshwar, B.
Hari Krishna, R.
Kirilova, Elena M.
Divakar, Darshan Devang
Alkheraif, Abdulaziz Abdullah
coredata.dc:description: The interaction of fluorescent probe 3-N-(N'-methylacetamidino) benzanthrone (3-MAB) with copper oxide and zinc oxide nanoparticles is studied, wherein the copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) and zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) were prepared by combustion method. The prepared CuO and ZnO nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) techniques. The interaction between fluorescent molecule 3-MAB and nanoparticles (CuO and ZnO) was investigated by absorption and fluorescence techniques. Further, the steady state fluorescence quenching of 3-MAB by CuO and ZnO nanoparticles in different solvents was studied at room temperature (298 K) and higher temperatures at 308 K and 318 K to determine the bimolecular quenching rate constant, binding constant and binding sites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence quenching
Benzanthrone
Nanoparticles
Binding constant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy
Interaction of 3-MAB with CuO and ZnO nanoparticles
Binding constant and number of binding sites:

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structure of 3-MAB along with IUPAC name.
PXRD of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles.
Scanning electron micrographs of (a) CuO (b) ZnO.
TEM images of (a) CuO (b) ZnO and HRTEM of (c) CuO (d) ZnO.

Fluorescence spectra of 3-MAB in the absence and presence of quencher (a) CuO nanoparticles in 1,4-dioxane solvent excited at 464 nm and (b) ZnO nanoparticles in 1,4-dioxane solvent excited at 464 nm.

(a) Stern-Volmer plots for 3-MAB in 1,4-dioxane at different temperatures with CuO as quencher. (b) Plot of 1/(Io-I) vs 1/[Q] as per Lineweaver-Burk relation for 3-MAB in 1,4-dioxane at different temperatures with CuO as quencher.

(a) Stern-Volmer plots for 3-MAB in 1,4-dioxane at different temperatures with ZnO as quencher. (b) Plot of 1/(Io-I) vs 1/[Q] as per Lineweaver-Burk relation for 3-MAB in 1,4-dioxane at different temperatures with ZnO as quencher.

Dependencies of log
I 0
- I
I
vs log [Q] for 3-MAB in solvents of different polarity with (a) CuO and (b) ZnO nanoparticles as quenchers measured at 298 K.

Lifetime (τo) and relative quantum yield (
∅ f
)
of 3-MAB in different solvents.

S-V constant (KSV), Lineweaver-Burk constant (KLB), bimolecular quenching rate parameter (kq), and binding constant (Ka) values obtained for 3-MAB in different solvents with CuO quencher at different temperatures.

S-V constant (KSV), Lineweaver-Burk constant (KLB), bimolecular quenching rate parameter (kq), and binding constant (Ka) values obtained for 3-MAB in different solvents with ZnO quencher at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Interaction of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles with 3-N-(N′-methylacetamidino) benzanthrone: A temperature dependent fluorescence quenching study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interaction of fluorescent probe 3-N-(N'-methylacetamidino) benzanthrone (3-MAB) with copper oxide and zinc oxide nanoparticles is studied, wherein the copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) and zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) were prepared by combustion method. The prepared CuO and ZnO nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) techniques. The interaction between fluorescent molecule 3-MAB and nanoparticles (CuO and ZnO) was investigated by absorption and fluorescence techniques. Further, the steady state fluorescence quenching of 3-MAB by CuO and ZnO nanoparticles in different solvents was studied at room temperature (298 K) and higher temperatures at 308 K and 318 K to determine the bimolecular quenching rate constant, binding constant and binding sites.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles have gained much importance over the past decade due to their hefty applications in various fields [1]. Among them, metal oxide nanoparticles have received much interest due to their various applications in diverse fields, such as medicine, sensing, catalysis and textile industry. Especially, copper oxide and zinc oxide nanoparticles are used in several consumer products and industrial processes [2,3]. Copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles are used in photoconductive and photothermal applications [4], gas sensors [5], wound healing materials [6] and face masks [7]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles are used as UV blockers in sun screen lotions, mixed varistor, solar cell, optoelectronics, and in gas sensors [8–9].

Excessive production, usage and disposal of these nanoparticles from consumer and household products into waste streams and further into the environment will pose threat to several creatures of this universe [10]. Understanding the toxicology of metal oxide nanoparticles is an area where research has progressed far [11]. But their detection and study of interaction with biological molecules is still in infant stage [12,13]. Among many of the analytical methods to detect metal oxide nanoparticles fluorescence techniques have gained lot of importance because of their higher success rates [14,15]. Also, fluorescence techniques are widely accepted due to their sensitivity and selectivity together with the advantages of spatial and temporal resolution. Further, the advances in fluorescence technology are decreasing the cost and complexity of experimental tools. Steady state and time resolved techniques can be used to gain information about the fluorophore and quencher conjugate systems, the interaction of molecules with their environment and the distribution of molecules interacting with their surroundings [16,17].

7H-Benzo[de]anthracen-7-one dye (commonly known as benzanthrone) is a well-known organic luminescent dye that emit in the spectral region from green to red due to variation in their structure and the substituent. Benzanthrone derivatives are potential fluorescent probes for detecting various chemical species in chemical and biological environments. The 3-substituted benzanthrone derivatives have been explored much by researchers because of their wide variety of applications. They have been used as a DNA intercalating solvatochromic fluorescent probe. [18]. They have also been used for the selective detection of copper ions [19]. Fluorescent benzanthrone derivatives were employed as pH sensor [20,21] and as optical sensor for detecting aliphatic amines and also as an antimicrobial agent [22,23].

In the present scenario with the frequent usage of CuO and ZnO in various applications it becomes essential to study the interaction of such nanoparticles with organic dyes. In order to understand the mechanism of interaction between CuO/ZnO nanoparticles with 3-MAB, we planned to perform detailed investigation of CuO/ZnO: 3-MAB association using absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The binding properties of 3-MAB with nanoparticles will be useful for understanding the reaction mechanism as well as for providing guidance for the design of new dyes for specific application. The molecular structure of the dye is as shown in Fig. 1 .

The dye 3-N-(N'-Methyl acetamidino) benzanthrone (3-MAB) was synthesized as described in the previous article [24]. The spectroscopic grade solvents 1,4-dioxane (Dio), toluene (Tl), ethyl acetate (EA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and dichloromethane (DCM), were obtained from S-D fine chemicals Ltd., (India) and used without any further purification.

The metal oxide nanoparticles (CuO and ZnO) were synthesised by low temperature initiated solution combustion synthesis. The method based on exothermic reaction between an oxidiser and fuel results in nanoparticle formation at relatively lower temperature compared to conventional techniques [25]. For CuO synthesis, stoichiometric quantities of copper nitrate and oxalyldihydrazide were mixed in a 300 ml crystalline dish. This mixture containing metal nitrate as oxidiser and an organic compound as fuel is termed as Redox mixture. To this redox mixture 30 ml of deionised water was added and stirred using magnetic stirrer for 15–20 min to form homogeneous mixture. A Petri dish containing this aqueous mixture was introduced to a pre-heated muffle furnace maintained at 400 °C. After ∼3–4 min, the solution underwent dehydration followed by the ignition of the redox mixture resulting in smouldering type combustion. The combustion process was accompanied by evolution of large amount of gaseous by products and finally ultra-fine voluminous CuO was obtained. The whole process was completed within 5–6 min and the sample was collected upon cooling the reaction vessel to the room temperature. The sample obtained was calcinated at 700 °C for 3 h in order to obtain the pure CuO crystalline phase.

Similarly, ZnO nano particles were also prepared by following similar protocol using zinc Nitrate and sugar as oxidiser and fuel, correspondingly. For the synthesis of ZnO the furnace temperature was maintained at 500 °C and the obtained product was white porous ZnO nanoparticles.

The synthesized CuO and ZnO nanoparticles were characterised through XRD, Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded in a Rigaku, SmartLab X-ray powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (
λ
= 0.15418 nm). CuO and ZnO sample was in the form of powder. A scan rate of 0.05° min−1 was applied to record the XRD pattern in the 2θ range of 20–80°, operated at 40 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) was employed for morphological study of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles using FESEM the ULTRA 55 Field emission scanning electron microscope operated at 5 kV. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was employed for particle size study of CuO, ZnO nanoparticles using JEOL JEM – 2100F operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

The spectral absorption measurements were recorded using Elico SL 159 UV–VIS spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurements were recorded using HITACHI F-2700 spectrofluorimeter, the excitation and emission slit widths (each 5 nm) and scan rate (1500 nm/min) and PMT voltage 700 V were maintained constant for all the measurements. The fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out using a Horiba Jobin Yvon TCSPC (time correlated single photon counting) lifetime instrument. The light source used was 370 nm Nano LED. 1 MHz pulse repetition rate and 370 nm LED with the instrumental full width at half-maximum is set, including ∼1.1 ns detector response. The Scatterer (Ludox AS40 colloidal silica) is used to collect instrument response function. IBH software is employed to measure the decay rate. The symmetrical distribution of the residuals was analyzed with a value of
χ
2
, 0.99 ≤
χ
2
≤ 1.2, to be considered as a good fit. By using the following equation (1) the average fluorescence lifetime (
τ
avg
) values were obtained [26]
(1)
τ
avg
=
∑
i = 1
n
α
i
τ
i
2
∑
i = 1
n
α
i
τ
i
where
τ i
is the individual lifetime with corresponding amplitude
α i
.

The powder X-ray diffraction pattern of CuO and ZnO obtained are shown in Fig. 2 (a and b) respectively. The pattern obtained for CuO nanoparticles matches with the standard JCPDS-80-1917. Since the sample was calcinated at 700 °C in open air atmosphere no other impurities such as Cu2O are detected in the XRD pattern. The high intense peaks without impurity phases confirm the formation of crystalline CuO. Further, the pattern seen in Fig. 2(b) corresponds to ZnO sample prepared using sugar as fuel. The diffraction peaks obtained can be indexed to wurtzite phase and matches with standard data JCPDS-70-0134 [27]. The XRD pattern are found to be intense with broad peaks. From the well resolved peaks with relatively higher FWHM it can be inferred that ZnO powder formed is highly nano crystalline by nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron micrographs of CuO and ZnO are shown in Fig. 3 (a and b) respectively. Surface morphology of CuO nanoparticles are found to be porous agglomerated mass with varying sizes of voids. Individual particles are not seen in the micrographs of CuO but all the particles are aggregated into particle clusters. It can also be noticed the presence of voids and pores in the structure that can be attributed to the liberation of gaseous by products during synthesis resulting in such porous structure [28]. However, on the other hand ZnO microstructural images show relatively lesser agglomeration and the particle clusters aggregated are found to be smaller than CuO sample. The voids and porous structural features are also seen in ZnO sample and it is typical of solution combustion process.

In order to accurately determine the particle size, distribution of CuO and ZnO, TEM characterisation was carried out and the images are presented in Fig. 4 (a and b). From the TEM images of CuO it is evident that the particles with size varying from 20 to 50 nm are agglomerated. The particles are interconnected to each other can be clearly seen and similar agglomerated particles are reported by many researchers previously for solution combustion derived samples. Fig. 4(b) shows the TEM images of ZnO and the particle size varies from 20 to 30 nm. The agglomerated particles can be seen forming cluster and this is in consistent with SEM results.

The relative quantum yield
∅ f
of 3-MAB is determined using the following equation (2) [26,29]. (Z)-N,N-diethyl-N′-(7-oxo-7H-benzo[de]anthracen-3-yl)acetamidine in toluene (
∅ r
= 0.56) is used as the reference molecule [30].
(2)
∅ f
=
∅ r
I f
A r
n
f
2
I r
A f
n
r
2
‘
∅
’ denotes the quantum yield, ‘I’ is the integral intensity, and ‘A’ is the area under the emission curve. The refractive index of solvents is represented by ‘n’; their values are taken from reference [31]. The subscript ‘f’ refers to the sample under consideration and ‘r’ to the reference molecule. The measured relative quantum yield
∅ f
and lifetime values (τo) of 3-MAB in different solvents are tabulated in table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of 3-MAB with CuO and ZnO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the interaction between 3-MAB and CuO/ZnO nanoparticles the fluorescence spectra of 3-MAB in different solvents is recorded in the presence of CuO/ZnO nanoparticles as quenchers. The solution was excited at 464 nm corresponding to the absorption maxima of 3-MAB. The concentration of 3-MAB was kept low (10−5 M) to avoid primary and secondary inner filter effects [32–35]. Concentration of quenchers CuO and ZnO nanoparticles were in the range of 0–1 mM. The fluorescence quenching data was analysed by using Stern-Volmer relation (S-V) (Eq. (3)) [26,32].
(3)
I
0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where
I
0
and
I
represents the emission intensities of solute in the absence and presence of quencher respectively. [Q] is the quencher concentration i.e of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles. KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. S-V constant and bimolecular quenching rate constant (kq) are related as shown in equation (4),
τ 0
is the excited state lifetime of solute.
(4)
K
S V
= k q
τ
0
,

The fluorescence intensity of 3-MAB in different solvents is measured in the presence of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles at room temperature (298 K). It was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing the concentration of CuO/ZnO nanoparticles. Fig. 5 (a) and (b) shows the effect of increasing concentration of CuO and ZnO nanoparticles on the fluorescence emission spectrum of 3-MAB in 1, 4-dioxane solvent respectively.

The plots of I0/I versus quencher concentration [Q] is plotted for both quenchers CuO and ZnO as shown in Figs. 6 a and 7 a respectively. Linear S-V plots are observed for both quenchers (in concentration range 0–1 mM) in all the solvents used in the present study. The S-V constant (KSV) obtained from the S-V plots are tabulated in tables 2 and 3
for both quenchers CuO and ZnO respectively. The linear S-V plot alone does not prove the existence of dynamic quenching, sometimes there is a possibility of static quenching resulting from linear S-V plots. Dynamic and static quenching can be distinguished by their differing dependence on temperature [26]. Therefore, we have repeated the fluorescence intensity measurements of 3-MAB in different solvents in the presence of both quenchers CuO and ZnO at higher temperatures 308 K and 318 K. We see that S-V plots are linear for 3-MAB with both CuO and ZnO quencher in all the solvents studied even at higher temperatures. With increase in temperature the linear curve obtained at room temperature (298 K) shifts towards the abscissa for both quenchers indicating the involvement of static quenching mechanism (As shown in Figs. 6a and 7a for 1,4-dioxane solvent) [26,33]. The fluorescence quenching data is analysed for different temperatures by S-V relation. The S-V constant (KSV) obtained at different temperatures for both quenchers CuO and ZnO are tabulated in tables 2 and 3. The KSV values decrease with increase in temperature indicates us to lean and stick to the fact that static quenching mechanism is involved in the present system. Besides, to check the ground state complex formation we invoked Lineweaver-Burk equation (5) to analyse the quenching data [34].
(5)
1
I o
- I
=
1
I o
+
1
K
LB
I o
[ Q ]

KLB is Lineweaver-Burk constant or apparent association constant and other terms in equation (5) have same meaning as explained in equation (3). KLB is obtained by plotting the graph of 1/(Io-I) versus 1/[Q] for different temperatures and tabulated in tables 2 and 3 for both quenchers CuO and ZnO respectively. Acceptable correlation coefficient is obtained for all cases [33]. In most of the cases the correlation coefficients are larger than 0.94 and indicate a linearity for the plots (Figs. 6b and 7b shows the plot for 3-MAB: CuO and 3-MAB: ZnO system in 1,4-dioxane at different temperatures respectively). With increase in temperature the KLB values decrease, this trend is seen in the above discussion with Ksv also, which indicates that an unstable complex has formed between 3-MAB and quenchers CuO/ZnO which dissociates at higher temperature [36–39].

Using τo values given in table 1, we have calculated kq values pertaining to Eq. (4) and are given in tables 2 and 3. Bimolecular quenching rate parameter (kq) values that are apparently larger than the diffusion controlled limit usually indicate some type of binding interaction that leads to static quenching. The higher values of kq obtained in the present systems reflects the higher efficiency of quenching. These values also infer that quenching is due to complex formation and not induced by dynamic collision [26,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding constant and number of binding sites:
ce:section.ce:para:
If independent binding of small molecules to a group of equivalent sites on a macromolecule is presumed then the equilibrium between free and bound molecules can be explained by Eq. (6).
(6)
l o g
I
0
- I
I
= l o g
[
K
a
]
+ n l o g
[ Q ]
where Ka is the binding constant of CuO/ ZnO nanoparticles with 3-MAB in different solvents, that can be determined from the intercept of log
I 0
- I
I
versus log [Q] curve. Such plots are shown in Fig. 8 a and b for 3-MAB with CuO and ZnO as quencher in different solvents at room temperature respectively. And ‘n’ is the number of binding sites on 3-MAB [39–41]. The obtained values of ‘Ka’ are summarized in tables 2 and 3 for investigated temperatures (298 K, 308 K, and 318 K). We notice the decrease in values of ‘Ka’ with increase in temperature. This further supports the fact that quenching is static in nature and it is because raise in temperature disfavours the binding interaction responsible for static quenching. Further, we notice that ‘n’ values were found to be nearly ≈1 and won’t change much with increase in temperature that infers the existence of one association site between 3-MAB and CuO/ZnO.

The interaction of 3-MAB with CuO and ZnO nanoparticles at different temperatures studied by absorption, and fluorescence spectroscopic measurements clearly indicates that CuO and ZnO nanoparticles quench the fluorescence of 3-MAB. The bimolecular quenching rate constant, binding constant and the number of binding sites at room temperature 298 K and higher temperatures 308 K, 318 K are obtained. Analyses of fluorescence quenching data with the help of Stern- Volmer and Lineweaver-Burk relations infer that static quenching is involved in the present system.

D. Bharathi: Investigation, Formal analysis. B. Siddlingeshwar: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – original draft. R. Hari Krishna: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Elena M. Kirilova: Investigation, Formal analysis. Darshan Devang Divakar: Validation, Formal analysis. Abdulaziz Abdullah Alkheraif: Validation, Supervision, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University for funding through Vice Deanship of Scientific Research Chairs.
